mainEntity.name: What's the difference between LRPS and ARPS?

acceptedAnswer.text: LRPS is entry-level: 10 images demonstrating solid technical skills, creative eye, variety of approach. Subjects can vary but cohesion required. ARPS is next level: 15 images forming a coherent body of work in a defined genre with personal voice and complete technical command. L assesses range; A assesses depth. Many ARPS candidates fail because they prepared with L-level thinking.

mainEntity.name: Do I need RPS membership to apply for a distinction?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. RPS distinctions are awarded only to current members of the Royal Photographic Society. Membership gives access to Advisory Days, the Distinctions handbook, and other resources useful as preparation.

mainEntity.name: How long does it take to prepare an RPS panel?

acceptedAnswer.text: Three to twelve months. Photographers with strong existing portfolios fitting the thesis can prepare in 3-6 months. Those needing new images or stepping up from L to A typically need 6-12 months. RPS mentoring runs over a 12-month timeline by default.

mainEntity.name: Should I attend an RPS Advisory Day?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, as one input among several rather than only preparation. Advisory Days give 10-15 minutes of assessor feedback — useful for catching specific issues but limited in scope. Treat as a checkpoint, not a substitute for sustained editorial work.

mainEntity.name: What happens if my panel fails?

acceptedAnswer.text: You receive written feedback detailing why the panel did not pass and what would need to change. No limit to resubmissions — many successful candidates pass on second or third attempt. Peter Orton's case study is exactly this scenario: failed first ARPS, structured rebuild, successful resubmission.

mainEntity.name: Do I need to print my panel, or can I submit digitally?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both options exist. Printed panels typically printed at A4 with consistent mounts. Digital submissions accepted for some routes but require fully calibrated projector. Most successful panels I've mentored have been printed. Check current RPS Distinctions handbook for specific submission options.

mainEntity.name: Is RPS mentoring with Alan Ranger officially RPS-accredited?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Mentoring is independent and not officially RPS-accredited or endorsed by the Society. Advice is private, based on holding both LRPS and ARPS distinctions and mentoring others through the process. The RPS itself runs Advisory Days and other support — complementary rather than competing.

mainEntity.name: How much does RPS mentoring cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most clients begin with a block of six one-hour Zoom sessions and add more as needed over twelve months. Sessions are flexible. Group classes available for up to three over Zoom. Current pricing on the RPS mentoring service page; free no-obligation initial chat offered before payment.
